senses.definition: To make someone foolish or infatuated, especially so they stop thinking clearly (now rare and old-fashioned).
examples: Old tales warn of magic that can assot even the wisest king.
He let his sudden success assot him and forgot his true friends.

senses.definition: Dazed or foolish because of strong feeling or desire (now rare and old-fashioned).
examples: In the poem he stands assot before her beauty.
She looked assot, lost between fear and hope.

senses.definition: Third person singular form of “assot”: makes someone foolish or infatuated (now rare and old-fashioned).
examples: The thought of sudden riches assots his judgment.
In the story, beauty assots him and leads him to ruin.

senses.definition: Plural of “assot” in the sense of foolish or infatuated people (very rare).
examples: The poet laughs at the assots who chase empty glory.
He felt they were all assots, blind to the truth.

senses.definition: Alternative spelling of “assot”: to make someone foolish or infatuated (now rare and old-fashioned).
examples: He feared that court life would assott his son.
The charm seemed to assott all who saw it.

senses.definition: Made foolish or stupefied, especially by strong feeling, pleasure, or drink (now rare and old-fashioned).
examples: He grew assotted with wine and praise.
In the novel she is assotted with a hopeless love.

senses.definition: Past tense of “assot”: made someone foolish or infatuated (now rare and old-fashioned).
examples: The promise of power assotted him.
Flattery assotted the young prince and led to his fall.

senses.definition: Present participle of “assot”: making someone foolish or infatuated (now rare and old-fashioned).
examples: Assotting the people with empty promises, the leader rose to power.
He blamed the city for assotting young minds with vain dreams.

senses.definition: Likely misspelling of “assembler”: a person or tool that puts parts together, especially in a factory or in computer programming.
examples: He worked for years as an assembler in a car plant.
The program is written in assembly language and translated by an assembler.

senses.definition: Written short form of “assistant,” used especially in job titles and notes.
examples: She was promoted from asst manager to manager last year.
Please contact the asst director if you have any questions.

senses.definition: Very rare spelling or error for “dissuade”: to persuade someone not to do something.
examples: Nothing could assuade him from taking that risky step.
They tried to assuade her, but she had already decided.

senses.definition: That can be made less strong or severe, especially about pain, fear, or guilt.
examples: Her grief was deep but still assuagable with time and support.
Some worries are not easily assuagable by simple answers.

senses.definition: To make an unpleasant feeling less strong or easier to bear.
examples: The apology helped assuage her anger.
He tried to assuage his guilt by making a donation.
A short walk in the fresh air can assuage stress.

senses.definition: To satisfy a need or desire enough to make it less strong.
examples: They brought food to assuage their hunger.
He checked his phone to assuage his curiosity.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of “assuage”: made a painful or unpleasant feeling less strong.
examples: Her worries were assuaged by the doctor’s calm explanation.
The letter assuaged his fears about the exam results.

senses.definition: The act of making something, especially an unpleasant feeling, less painful or severe.
examples: He sought the assuagement of his guilt through honest confession.
Music brought some assuagement of her sadness.

senses.definition: Plural of “assuagement”: actions or things that make unpleasant feelings less strong.
examples: The different assuagements of pain included rest, medicine, and gentle exercise.
Poetry and prayer were his main assuagements in hard times.

senses.definition: Someone or something that reduces pain, worry, or another unpleasant feeling.
examples: The nurse became an assuager of fear for many patients.
For him, music was the greatest assuager of loneliness.

senses.definition: Plural of “assuager”: people or things that make unpleasant feelings less strong.
examples: The volunteers were assuagers of fear in the disaster zone.
Books and friends are often quiet assuagers of stress.

senses.definition: Third person singular form of “assuage”: makes a bad feeling less strong.
examples: The doctor’s calm voice assuages many patients’ fears.
A quick phone call often assuages their worries.

senses.definition: Present participle of “assuage”: making an unpleasant feeling less strong.
examples: She focused on assuaging the child’s fear of the dark.
Assuaging public concern became the government’s main task.

senses.definition: Very rare plural form referring to acts of making something less painful or severe.
examples: Through many small assuagings, he rebuilt his broken confidence.
Their kindnesses were quiet assuagings of her constant worry.

senses.definition: Able to calm, soften, or make a bad feeling less strong.
examples: His assuasive words helped calm the angry crowd.
The medicine has an assuasive effect on the pain.

senses.definition: Very rare form meaning to bring someone or something under complete control or subjection.
examples: The invaders sought to assubjugate the whole region.
He refused to assubjugate his will to unjust orders.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of “assubjugate”: brought under complete control or oppression.
examples: The region was assubjugated by foreign powers.
He would not be assubjugated by threats.

senses.definition: Third person singular form of “assubjugate”: brings someone or something under complete control.
examples: The regime assubjugates dissenting voices through fear.
A toxic relationship slowly assubjugates a person’s sense of self.

senses.definition: Present participle of “assubjugate”: bringing someone or something under very strong control or power.
examples: They were assubjugating the local culture with strict new rules.
Assubjugating whole communities through debt is a modern form of control.

senses.definition: The process of becoming used to something, especially a drug or repeated influence (very formal and rare).
examples: Long-term use can lead to assuefaction and dependence.
The essay discussed the assuefaction of society to constant noise.

senses.definition: Plural of “assuefaction”: instances or processes of becoming used to something, especially through repeated exposure.
examples: The study described various assuefactions to stress in the workplace.
Social media use can create powerful assuefactions among teenagers.

senses.definition: The state of being used to something; a settled habit, especially one formed over a long time.
examples: He lived in quiet assuetude to his daily routines.
There was an assuetude of hard work in that family.

senses.definition: Plural of “assuetude”: long‑standing habits or states of being used to certain conditions.
examples: Different cultures develop different assuetudes of behavior.
His many assuetudes made it hard for him to change his lifestyle.

senses.definition: Plural of “assumability”: the qualities or conditions that make something possible to take on or accept.
examples: The report analyzed the assumabilities of different risks.
Legal and financial assumabilities must both be considered.

senses.definition: The quality of something that can be taken on, accepted, or supposed.
examples: The assumability of such costs is still in question.
We must check the loan’s assumability before buying the house.

senses.definition: Able to be taken on, accepted, or supposed.
examples: The remaining debt is not assumable by the buyer.
It is assumable that the project will finish on time.

senses.definition: Used to say that something is probably true, based on what seems reasonable.
examples: He will, assumably, arrive before noon.
The documents were, assumably, lost in the move.

senses.definition: To accept something as true without proof or checking.
examples: I assumed he was coming, so I saved him a seat.
Let’s not assume that she knows about the change.
People often assume too much from a short message.

senses.definition: To start to have a position, duty, or form.
examples: She will assume the role of director next month.
The company assumed full responsibility for the error.

senses.definition: To pretend to have a particular appearance or quality.
examples: He assumed a calm expression, though he was nervous.
The actor assumed an air of confidence.

senses.definition: Accepted as true or real, often without proof.
examples: They acted on the basis of assumed facts.
The story is set in an assumed future world.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of “assume”: took something as true, or took on a role or duty.
examples: We assumed the meeting was canceled.
She assumed control of the project last year.

senses.definition: As is supposed or believed, often without proof.
examples: He is assumedly the best candidate for the job.
The house was assumedly empty when they entered.

senses.definition: Taking on or assuming something; characterized by assumption (very rare).
examples: The assument party accepts all future costs.
Assument powers were granted to the temporary council.

senses.definition: A person or organization that takes something as true or takes on a duty or responsibility.
examples: The assumer of the debt must sign the contract.
As the assumer of these tasks, she needs full information.

senses.definition: Plural of “assumer”: people or organizations that take something as true or take on duties or responsibilities.
examples: The assumers of risk are clearly named in the contract.
New assumers of the lease must be approved by the landlord.

senses.definition: Third person singular form of “assume”: accepts something as true without proof, or takes on a role or duty.
examples: She always assumes the best in people.
The company assumes all travel costs.
He assumes the role of leader when the manager is away.

senses.definition: Used to say that something will happen or be true only if another thing happens or is true.
examples: Assuming the weather is good, we will go hiking.
Assuming you agree, we can sign the contract today.

senses.definition: Behaving as if you have more importance or right than you really do.
examples: I found his tone rather assuming for a new employee.
Her assuming manner annoyed the rest of the team.

senses.definition: Present participle of “assume”: taking something as true, or taking on a role or duty.
examples: You are assuming too much from her short message.
He is assuming control of the department next month.

senses.definition: In a way that shows someone is taking something for granted or acting as if they have more right than they do.
examples: He assumingly walked in as if he owned the place.
They assumingly expect everyone to work late.

senses.definition: The quality of behaving as if you have more right or importance than you really do.
examples: His assumingness made him unpopular with colleagues.
There is a certain assumingness in the way she speaks to others.

senses.definition: Very rare plural form referring to different acts or states of assuming something.
examples: Their many assumings led to serious misunderstandings.
These assumings about others’ motives are dangerous.

senses.definition: Extremely rare or erroneous form possibly intended as “summon”: to call someone to come, especially officially.
examples: The clerk moved to assummon the witness to the stand.
He tried to assummon the courage to speak.

senses.definition: In older law, a legal action to claim money for breaking a promise or contract that was not under seal.
examples: The plaintiff brought an action in assumpsit to recover damages.
Historically, assumpsit was used to enforce informal agreements.

senses.definition: A promise or agreement that can be the basis for such an action in law.
examples: The court found an implied assumpsit in the parties’ conduct.
An express assumpsit was written into the document.

senses.definition: Plural of “assumpsit”: legal actions or promises of the kind recognized in this older form of law.
examples: The textbook explains the different types of assumpsits used at common law.
Several assumpsits were filed against the company in the 18th century.

senses.definition: Taken for granted or supposed; assumed (very rare and mainly literary).
examples: The story rests on an assumpt truth about human nature.
His arguments depend on several assumpt principles.

senses.definition: Something that you accept as true without proof.
examples: Many people make the assumption that money brings happiness.
Our plan is based on the assumption that sales will rise.
It is a common assumption that he will win.

senses.definition: The act of taking on a duty, power, or position.
examples: His assumption of office was delayed.
The assumption of responsibility comes with extra stress.

senses.definition: A member of a Catholic religious order called the Augustinians of the Assumption (usually capitalized).
examples: The Assumptionist worked as a teacher in the local school.
Several Assumptionists were present at the conference.

senses.definition: Plural of “assumption”: things that are accepted as true without proof.
examples: We need to question our basic assumptions.
Their argument depends on several doubtful assumptions.

senses.definition: Behaving as if you have the right to do things without proof or permission; very presumptuous (rare).
examples: It was rather assumptious of him to speak for the whole group.
Her assumptious remarks upset many listeners.
